</imports>

<findings>

[observation] [warning] 22 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
[observation] [warning] High coupling (0.56) — 33 imports target "remote-integration"
[observation] [info] 49 inbound imports from PRD Analysis Pipeline and 35 from the CLI zone confirm this is the highest-leverage zone in the package; regressions here will ripple widely.
[observation] [info] Cohesion at 0.44 is acceptable but the zone spans schema definitions, core business logic, and CLI entry points simultaneously — consider whether schema and contracts deserve their own zone as the package grows.
[observation] [info] sync-engine.ts and sync.ts co-existing in the same zone as local core utilities may introduce accidental coupling between local and remote concerns.
[suggestion] [info] Zone "prd-operations-core" has files across 9 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
[suggestion] [info] The parallel src/core/X.ts + src/cli/commands/X.ts naming pattern (fix/fix, next-task/next, health/health) is unenforced: some CLI commands have core counterparts and others do not, with no rule determining which deserve the split — document the criterion (e.g. 'core module required when logic has 2+ consumers or is tested independently') to prevent inconsistent future additions
[suggestion] [warning] core/fix.ts has exactly one non-test consumer (cli/commands/fix.ts) — a single-consumer core module violates the reuse rationale for the core layer; evaluate whether fix logic should live directly in the CLI command or remain in core only if future consumers are planned
[relationship] [critical] prd-operations-core imports constants.ts from rex-status-mcp-cli (a higher-layer zone), creating an upward dependency that inverts the intended domain→CLI layering; constants.ts should be extracted to a shared foundation layer accessible to both zones without hierarchy violation
[anti-pattern] [warning] SyncEngine is defined in src/core/sync-engine.ts (core layer) but re-exported from src/store/index.ts (store layer). Consumers importing SyncEngine via store/index.ts form a dependency on the store layer when they actually need a core concept, and the store gateway now implicitly exposes a core internal. SyncEngine should either live in the store layer or be exported directly from a core barrel.
[anti-pattern] [warning] src/core/ contains 32 modules with no index.ts — all 32 internal filenames are implicit public API. Any consumer (mcp-tools.ts, CLI commands, hench gateway) directly path-imports internal modules. There is no mechanism to distinguish public core operations from private implementation helpers, making the core zone's stability contract invisible and unenforceable.

</findings>

<insights>

- High coupling (0.56) — 33 imports target "remote-integration"
- 22 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
- This zone is the gravity well of the rex package — nearly every other zone imports from it, making it the critical path for stability
- The mix of schema types (v1.ts, contracts.ts), core utilities (merge, prune, sync), and CLI commands (add, reorganize) suggests some further decomposition could improve clarity
- public.ts living here anchors the package's external API contract and should be treated as a stability boundary
- Cohesion at 0.44 is acceptable but the zone spans schema definitions, core business logic, and CLI entry points simultaneously — consider whether schema and contracts deserve their own zone as the package grows.
- 49 inbound imports from PRD Analysis Pipeline and 35 from the CLI zone confirm this is the highest-leverage zone in the package; regressions here will ripple widely.
- sync-engine.ts and sync.ts co-existing in the same zone as local core utilities may introduce accidental coupling between local and remote concerns.
- Zone "prd-operations-core" has files across 9 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
- constants.ts lives inside rex-status-mcp-cli but is imported by 7 zones including prd-operations-core, meaning the core domain zone has an upward dependency on a CLI/MCP zone — a layer inversion
- prd-operations-core imports constants.ts from rex-status-mcp-cli (a higher-layer zone), creating an upward dependency that inverts the intended domain→CLI layering; constants.ts should be extracted to a shared foundation layer accessible to both zones without hierarchy violation
- src/core/ has 32 files with no index.ts barrel — every consumer must import core modules by their internal path names, making the 32 internal filenames an implicit API contract. Without a barrel, the core zone's intended public surface is invisible and unenforceable: a module added for internal use is immediately importable by any consumer without any signal that it is private
- SyncEngine (core/sync-engine.ts) lives in the core layer but is re-exported through store/index.ts alongside store-layer types (FileStore, NotionStore, AdapterRegistry) — this makes SyncEngine appear to be part of the store's public API while physically residing in core, blurring the core/store boundary and making the zone membership of SyncEngine ambiguous to consumers
- SyncEngine is defined in src/core/sync-engine.ts (core layer) but re-exported from src/store/index.ts (store layer). Consumers importing SyncEngine via store/index.ts form a dependency on the store layer when they actually need a core concept, and the store gateway now implicitly exposes a core internal. SyncEngine should either live in the store layer or be exported directly from a core barrel.
- src/core/ contains 32 modules with no index.ts — all 32 internal filenames are implicit public API. Any consumer (mcp-tools.ts, CLI commands, hench gateway) directly path-imports internal modules. There is no mechanism to distinguish public core operations from private implementation helpers, making the core zone's stability contract invisible and unenforceable.
- No documented policy distinguishes which core operations merit their own module versus living inline in a CLI command — the result is inconsistent abstraction depth: some concepts (fix, next-task) have a dedicated core module even when only one CLI consumer exists, while others embed domain logic directly in commands
- core/fix.ts has exactly one non-test consumer (cli/commands/fix.ts) — a single-consumer core module violates the reuse rationale for the core layer; evaluate whether fix logic should live directly in the CLI command or remain in core only if future consumers are planned
- The parallel src/core/X.ts + src/cli/commands/X.ts naming pattern (fix/fix, next-task/next, health/health) is unenforced: some CLI commands have core counterparts and others do not, with no rule determining which deserve the split — document the criterion (e.g. 'core module required when logic has 2+ consumers or is tested independently') to prevent inconsistent future additions
- [call graph] 1243 internal calls, 158 outgoing, 109 incoming (cohesion: 0.89, coupling: 0.11)

</insights>
